justification: Malformed ML-KEM AlgorithmIdentifier breaks PKIX interoperability at issuance and consumption time.
ML-KEM certificate keyUsage is an issuance policy requirement and should block misprofiled certificates.
Wrong ML-KEM public-key length is a structural defect that indicates malformed SPKI encoding.
Unreduced ML-KEM encoded keys are structurally suspicious and should at least surface operationally until local detection is implemented.
ML-KEM seed-only private keys must match the RFC container size before import proceeds.
ML-KEM expanded private keys must match parameter-set sizes before import proceeds.
A both-form ML-KEM private key must be rejected on seed/expanded mismatch during import.
Expanded ML-KEM private keys require the FIPS/RFC hash check before use.
Malformed ML-DSA AlgorithmIdentifier breaks PKIX interoperability at issuance and validation time.
ML-DSA certificate signatureAlgorithm encoding is a PKIX conformance requirement and should block issuance defects.
ML-DSA certificates need a permitted signing usage when keyUsage is present.
ML-DSA certificates must not advertise encipherment or agreement semantics they cannot safely support.
Wrong ML-DSA public-key length is a structural defect that indicates malformed SPKI encoding.
HashML-DSA is prohibited in the covered PKIX certificate contexts and should always block.
ML-DSA seed-only private keys must match the RFC container size before import proceeds.
ML-DSA expanded private keys must match parameter-set sizes before import proceeds.
A both-form ML-DSA private key must be rejected on seed/expanded mismatch during import.
